serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried pork cartilage is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small plate portion as commonly served in Vietnamese street food or hawker settings.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Pork Cartilage (Dồi Sụn Chiên)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore — Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database entry for Fried Pork Cartilage (Dồi Sụn Chiên), record ID G00010581, with a recorded confidence of 0.95. All per_100g core macro and micronutrient values are taken from this canonical HPB entry. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) is an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for deep-fried pork variety meats, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately for this entry; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fried pork cartilage contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB Foodo database (G00010581). Per University of Sydney GI Database methodology, glycemic index testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate content; foods with negligible or zero carbohydrate are classified as not applicable. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition (GL = GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100; carbohydrate = 0 g). This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance that pure protein-and-fat foods do not elicit a measurable glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g threshold; deep-frying also contributes saturated fat that promotes arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit fried pork cartilage due to critically elevated sodium (1050 mg/100 g); if consumed, restrict to a very small portion (≤30 g) and avoid additional high-sodium condiments in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content supports glycaemic stability, but deep-frying adds significant fat that worsens insulin resistance over time; high sodium also elevates cardiovascular risk in diabetic patients.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried pork cartilage has negligible carbohydrate and will not cause acute glycaemic spikes, but patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size due to high fat from deep-frying and critically high sodium (1050 mg/100 g) that compounds cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100 g), elevated cholesterol (89 mg/100 g), saturated fat from deep-frying, and potential trans fat formation during frying collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid fried pork cartilage due to the combination of critically high sodium, elevated cholesterol, and saturated fat from deep-frying, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiac events.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; pork is a red meat with pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid; high sodium worsens tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid fried pork cartilage as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, pork contributes arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways, and sodium at 1050 mg/100 g exacerbates joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Pork Cartilage, known in Vietnamese as Dồi Sụn Chiên, is a deep-fried pork dish made from pork cartilage that is seasoned with salt and fish sauce before being submerged in hot oil until crispy and golden. The cartilage yields a distinctive chewy-yet-crunchy texture that differentiates it from standard pork cuts, with a savoury, slightly salty flavour profile intensified by the fish sauce marinade. It is a popular Vietnamese street food and hawker dish found in Singapore's Vietnamese food stalls, typically served as a side dish or snack alongside rice or noodle-based meals at lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food. However, sodium is critically elevated at 1050 mg/100 g due to salt and fish sauce seasoning, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The deep-frying process adds saturated fat and may generate trans fats and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100 g. This dish is not suitable for patients managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis, and should be consumed with caution by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the high fat and sodium content.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat fried pork cartilage?
Is fried pork cartilage high in sodium?
Is fried pork cartilage suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010581) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is a primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. The food identity, Vietnamese cuisine classification, and nutrient profile are all consistent and internally coherent.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database (G00010581); this food is a pure protein-and-fat product with no starch or sugar component — confirmed zero. sugar_g is confirmed zero per the same source. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as pork cartilage contains no plant fibre — naturally zero. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g per HPB); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per low/zero-carbohydrate GI rule. saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.2 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for deep-fried pork variety meats, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately for this entry; this is an adapted estimate documented in nutrient_source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo database for this entry and not available in a directly matching approved source as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this specific dish and no directly matching approved-source entry provides complete micronutrient coverage for fried pork cartilage as of 2026-04-16.
